content.text: defines how the crew's tasks are scheduled and which agent owns each step. It controls the answer to:
(the crew decides at design time) and

attrs.alt: Sequential vs hierarchical process comparison diagram

content.text: Sequential is the default. Tasks run in the exact order they appear in the
list. Each task is assigned to the agent named in
. Context flows forward: a task can declare
to receive the earlier result as part of its prompt.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

planner = Agent(role="Planner", goal="Outline the article structure", backstory="...")
writer  = Agent(role="Writer",  goal="Write the article", backstory="...")
editor  = Agent(role="Editor",  goal="Polish the final draft", backstory="...")

plan_task = Task(
    description="Create a detailed outline for an article on {topic}",
    expected_output="A numbered outline with section titles and key points",
    agent=planner,
)
write_task = Task(
    description="Write the article following the provided outline",
    expected_output="A 700-word first draft",
    agent=writer,
    context=[plan_task],
)
edit_task = Task(
    description="Proofread and improve the draft",
    expected_output="A polished, publication-ready article",
    agent=editor,
    context=[write_task],
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[planner, writer, editor],
    tasks=[plan_task, write_task, edit_task],
    process=Process.sequential,  # default — can be omitted
    verbose=True,
)
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "quantum computing in 2024"})
print(result.raw)
The task list order is the execution order. If you list
, the editor runs before the writer — CrewAI does not reorder based on context dependencies.
Tip: always list tasks in dependency order under
to pass results forward, but the list order determines when each runs.

Hierarchical mode adds a manager agent that sits above the specialist agents. The manager receives the crew goal, breaks it into sub-tasks, dispatches them to the right agents, optionally in parallel, and synthesises the final result.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Specialist agents — no manager here
researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="Gather information", backstory="...")
analyst    = Agent(role="Analyst",    goal="Interpret data",    backstory="...")
writer     = Agent(role="Writer",     goal="Write reports",     backstory="...")

# Tasks — no strict order required; manager decides
gather_task = Task(
    description="Gather all available data on {topic}",
    expected_output="Raw data and sources",
    agent=researcher,
)
analyse_task = Task(
    description="Analyse the gathered data and identify trends",
    expected_output="List of trends with supporting figures",
    agent=analyst,
)
report_task = Task(
    description="Write an executive summary report",
    expected_output="A two-page executive summary",
    agent=writer,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[gather_task, analyse_task, report_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),  # auto-creates manager agent
    verbose=True,
)

result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "AI adoption in healthcare"})
print(result.raw)
Instead of letting CrewAI auto-generate a manager from
. This lets you give the manager a persona, backstory, and tools of its own.

from crewai import Agent, Crew, Process

manager = Agent(
    role="Senior Project Manager",
    goal="Coordinate the team and deliver high-quality output on deadline",
    backstory=(
        "A decade of managing cross-functional research teams. "
        "Known for clear communication and ruthless prioritisation."
    ),
    allow_delegation=True,
    verbose=True,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[gather_task, analyse_task, report_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_agent=manager,
)
Do NOT add the manager to the
list. CrewAI automatically inserts it; listing it twice causes duplicate dispatching.
Sequential vs Hierarchical — When to Use Each
N + M calls (N tasks + M planning)
is the only way to pass results between tasks. A task's prompt is augmented with the full text output of each listed context task.
, the manager agent automatically accumulates results and passes relevant context when it dispatches each new task — you rarely need to set

# Sequential: explicit context wiring required
write_task = Task(
    description="Write the article",
    expected_output="700-word draft",
    agent=writer,
    context=[plan_task, research_task],  # both results injected into prompt
)

# Hierarchical: manager handles context routing automatically
# context=[] is fine — the manager knows what tasks have run
. You can adjust its behaviour by supplying a

from crewai_tools import SerperDevTool

manager = Agent(
    role="Research Director",
    goal="Deliver a comprehensive, well-sourced report",
    backstory="Director who validates sources personally before passing work downstream.",
    tools=[SerperDevTool()],   # manager can search the web directly
    allow_delegation=True,
    max_iter=8,                # limit planning loops
    verbose=True,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[...],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_agent=manager,
)

on a sequential crew adds a preliminary planning step. A planner LLM analyses all tasks and produces an execution plan before any agent runs. This does not change the process mode — tasks still execute sequentially — but the agents receive more structured instructions.

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
    planning=True,
    planning_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),  # cheaper model for planning
    verbose=True,
)
Use planning when task descriptions are ambiguous and you want the LLM to resolve them before execution, not during.
for debug-level output) to see the manager's delegation decisions, which agent handles each task, and the full conversation history.

import os
os.environ["CREWAI_LOG_LEVEL"] = "DEBUG"   # even more granular

crew = Crew(..., verbose=2)
result = crew.kickoff()
to your observability stack (Langfuse, Arize, etc.) instead of relying on console output.
Choosing the Right Process — Decision Guide
Can tasks run in any order or in parallel?
Do you know exactly which agent handles each task?
If yes → sequential. If you want the manager to match tasks to agents → hierarchical.
Hierarchical adds manager overhead (extra LLM calls). Sequential has none.
Is the workflow exploratory or research-heavy?
Hierarchical shines here — the manager can adaptively spawn new sub-tasks based on what it discovers.
Both process modes expose per-task and per-agent guardrails to prevent runaway loops:

# Per-agent: cap how many reasoning iterations an agent performs
researcher = Agent(
    role="Researcher",
    goal="Find data",
    backstory="...",
    max_iter=5,           # default 25 — reduce for constrained tasks
    max_rpm=10,           # per-agent rate limit (requests/min)
    max_execution_time=60 # seconds — hard wall for a single agent turn
)

# Per-crew: cap LLM calls across all agents
crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher],
    tasks=[task],
    max_rpm=30,     # combined cap for the whole crew
)
applies to the manager agent as well. A tight manager
can cause it to stop dispatching tasks early — set it higher than the number of tasks you expect.
Both process modes support Pydantic outputs on individual tasks. Under hierarchical mode, the manager reads the structured output from each completed task and incorporates it into its planning.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List

class Finding(BaseModel):
    headline: str
    supporting_data: str
    confidence: float  # 0.0–1.0

class ResearchOutput(BaseModel):
    topic: str
    findings: List[Finding]
    sources: List[str]

research_task = Task(
    description="Research {topic} and return structured findings",
    expected_output="A JSON object with topic, findings list, and sources",
    agent=researcher,
    output_pydantic=ResearchOutput,
)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, writer], tasks=[research_task, write_task], process=Process.sequential)
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "AI regulation"})

# Access typed output
structured: ResearchOutput = research_task.output.pydantic
for f in structured.findings:
    print(f"{f.headline} (confidence={f.confidence})")
Even in sequential mode, individual agents can delegate sub-tasks to other agents within the same crew if
. This is distinct from the manager in hierarchical mode — here, a specialist agent ad-hoc asks another specialist for help.

researcher = Agent(
    role="Lead Researcher",
    goal="Produce a complete research report",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=True,  # may ask writer for a draft mid-research
)
writer = Agent(
    role="Writer",
    goal="Produce clear, well-structured prose",
    backstory="...",
    allow_delegation=False,  # stays focused, does not delegate further
)

# With sequential process — researcher may still call writer during its task
crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, publish_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
)
Delegation in sequential mode uses the same
as hierarchical mode. The key difference: in sequential mode
If an agent fails (exception, token exhaustion, guardrail rejection), CrewAI by default raises the error and halts the crew. You can configure
at the task level to retry automatically.
For production deployments, connect CrewAI to an observability platform by setting LangChain callbacks on each agent's LLM. The
writes a plain-text transcript of every agent exchange.

import os

# Arize Phoenix (open-source observability)
# pip install arize-phoenix-otel
from phoenix.otel import register
tracer_provider = register(project_name="crewai-run")

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
    output_log_file="logs/crew_run.txt",  # human-readable transcript
    verbose=True,
)
Create multiple independent research tasks with no
interdependency, then one synthesis task. Under hierarchical mode the manager dispatches the research tasks in parallel and waits for all to complete before running synthesis.

research_us = Task(description="Research AI regulation in the US",
    expected_output="Key US regulations and bodies", agent=researcher_us)
research_eu = Task(description="Research AI regulation in the EU",
    expected_output="Key EU regulations and bodies", agent=researcher_eu)
research_cn = Task(description="Research AI regulation in China",
    expected_output="Key Chinese regulations and bodies", agent=researcher_cn)

synthesis = Task(
    description="Compare and contrast AI regulation across US, EU, and China",
    expected_output="A comparative analysis table plus key insights",
    agent=analyst,
    # No context needed — manager passes all three results automatically
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher_us, researcher_eu, researcher_cn, analyst],
    tasks=[research_us, research_eu, research_cn, synthesis],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
)

Insert a validation task between generation and publication. The validator reads the draft and either approves it (short output) or rejects it with specific feedback. Downstream tasks receive that feedback via

validate_task = Task(
    description=(
        "Review the draft strictly. If it meets the style guide, output only 'APPROVED'. "
        "If not, output 'REJECTED: ' followed by specific numbered issues."
    ),
    expected_output="APPROVED or REJECTED: <issues>",
    agent=editor,
    context=[write_task],
)
revise_task = Task(
    description=(
        "If the review says APPROVED, output the draft unchanged. "
        "Otherwise revise it to address every listed issue."
    ),
    expected_output="Final polished draft",
    agent=writer,
    context=[write_task, validate_task],
)
(default) runs tasks in list order — deterministic, simple, and low-overhead.
uses a manager agent that dynamically dispatches, can parallelise, and synthesises results — powerful but adds LLM call overhead.
for a hand-crafted one with custom tools and persona.
to pass results forward; under hierarchical, the manager routes context automatically.
adds a pre-execution planning step to sequential crews — resolves ambiguous instructions before any agent runs.
Default to sequential; switch to hierarchical only when the workflow genuinely needs dynamic dispatch or parallelism.
